text:
REFINED BRIEFING (events grouped by probative direction).
Retrieval hints: actors=['Donald Trump', 'Canada', 'Mexico', 'United States'], topics=['tariffs', 'trade policy', 'north america', 'imports'].

## SUPPORTS-YES (12)
- [2025-02-01] On February 1, 2025, U.S. President Donald Trump signed orders imposing tariffs on goods imported from Canada and Mexico. The orders mandated 25% tariffs on most imports from both countries, with a 10% tariff specifically on oil and energy imports from Canada. In response, then-Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced Canada's intention to impose 25% tariffs on CA$30 billion (US$20.6 billion) of American goods, with plans to increase to CA$155 billion (US$106 billion) after three weeks. Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum also stated Mexico would implement retaliatory tariffs and non-tariff measures. A one-month delay for the tariffs was negotiated on February 3. The U.S. tariffs took effect on March 4, with Canada's retaliatory tariffs beginning simultaneously. Mexico stated it would delay its response. On March 6, Trump exempted goods compliant with the United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA) from the tariffs.
- [2025-02-01] On February 1, 2025, President Donald J. Trump signed three executive orders imposing additional tariffs on products from Canada, Mexico, and China. The order for Canada included an additional 25% tariff on most products and 10% on energy resources. Tariffs on Mexico included an additional 25% on all products, while tariffs on China's products were set at an additional 10%. The tariffs on China went into effect on February 4, 2025. The tariffs on Canada and Mexico, also scheduled for February 4, 2025, were suspended for one month after phone calls between President Trump and the leaders of those two countries. The tariffs are in addition to existing tariffs, do not include an exclusion process, and are imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEPPA).
- [2025-02-01] President Donald J. Trump issued an order on February 1, 2025, imposing an additional 25 percent ad valorem duty on all articles that are products of Mexico. The duties are scheduled to apply to goods entered for consumption on or after 12:01 a.m. eastern time on February 4, 2025. The action is taken under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and the National Emergencies Act (NEA), expanding the scope of a national emergency declared on January 20, 2025 (Proclamation 10886). Trump stated that Mexico's failure to intercept drug trafficking organizations, human traffickers, and illicit drugs constitutes an "unusual and extraordinary threat" to U.S. national security, foreign policy, and economy. The Secretary of Homeland Security is directed to make necessary modifications to the Harmonized Tariff Schedule of the United States (HTSUS) and to consult with other officials. The order states the tariffs can be removed if the government of Mexico takes adequate steps to alleviate illegal migration and illicit drug issues. The order also states that the President may increase or expand the duties if Mexico retaliates.
- [2025-02-04] On February 4, 2025, the Trump Administration's 10% additional tariffs on goods from China took effect. The tariffs apply to all Chinese goods, with limited exemptions, and became effective at 12:01 a.m. on February 4, 2025. The measures were initially imposed via Executive Orders pursuant to the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to address concerns related to immigration and drug trafficking. On February 3, 2025, agreements with Mexico and Canada delayed the implementation of tariffs on their goods for one month, shifting the effective date from February 4 to March 4. New Executive Orders were issued to implement these postponements. In response to the implemented tariffs, China announced retaliatory measures, including tariffs of up to 15% on U.S. energy imports and the addition of five critical minerals to its export controls list.
- [2025-02-02] President Trump stated on Sunday, February 2, 2025, that the Federal Reserve was correct in keeping interest rates unchanged at its policy meeting on Wednesday, January 29, 2025. This followed his January 23, 2025, comments advocating for lower rates. Trump also announced plans over the weekend to implement 25% tariffs on most Mexican and Canadian goods, a 10% tariff on Canadian oil, and a 10% tariff on Chinese imports, effective Tuesday, February 4, 2025. On Monday, February 3, 2025, he declared a one-month pause on the Mexican tariffs after Mexico agreed to deploy 10,000 troops to the border to address fentanyl and illegal migration. Trump said tariffs could cause "short-term pain" but would provide long-term benefits for the U.S. economy.
- [2025-02-01] On February 1, 2025, President Donald Trump announced tariffs on Canadian goods. The measures included a 25% tariff on most imports and a 10% tariff on Canadian energy products, with sanctions set to begin on March 4. Trump signed three executive orders and a Fact Sheet under the 1977 International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), declaring a national emergency. He cited an "extraordinary threat posed by illegal aliens and drugs" and Canada's "failures" to address narcotics flow. In response, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced 25% tariffs on US products, valued at CA$155 billion (US$107 billion), escalating trade tensions between the two nations.
